infobox.value: March 27, 1794; 232 years ago (1794-03-27) (in current form)  ---   October 13, 1775; 250 years ago (1775-10-13) (as the [Continental Navy](/wiki/Continental_Navy "Continental Navy"))[[1]](#cite_note-Establishment_of_the_Navy,_13_October_1775-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)   ---

(Redirected from [US Navy](/w/index.php?title=US_Navy&redirect=no "US Navy"))

Maritime service branch of the U.S. military

"USN" redirects here. For other uses, see [USN (disambiguation)](/wiki/USN_(disambiguation) "USN (disambiguation)").

For civilian military department for naval forces, see [United States Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy").

The **United States Navy** (**USN**) is the [maritime](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare") [service branch](/wiki/Military_branch "Military branch") of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") and is designated as the [navy](/wiki/Navy "Navy") of the United States in the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"). With 290 combat vessels, it is the world's second largest navy, behind the [People's Liberation Army Navy](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Navy "People's Liberation Army Navy"),[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8) and by far the largest by [displacement](/wiki/Displacement_(ship) "Displacement (ship)"), at 4.5 million tons in 2021.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It has the world's largest [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") fleet, with [eleven in service](/wiki/List_of_aircraft_carriers_in_service "List of aircraft carriers in service"), one undergoing trials, two new carriers under construction, and six other carriers planned as of 2024. The U.S. Navy is a part of the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") and is one of six armed forces of the United States and one of eight [uniformed services of the United States](/wiki/Uniformed_services_of_the_United_States "Uniformed services of the United States").

The Navy originates from the [Continental Navy](/wiki/Continental_Navy "Continental Navy"), established in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The [Naval Act of 1794](/wiki/Naval_Act_of_1794 "Naval Act of 1794") provided for constructing [six heavy frigates](/wiki/Original_six_frigates_of_the_United_States_Navy "Original six frigates of the United States Navy"), the first ships of the Navy, in the prelude to the [Barbary Wars](/wiki/Barbary_Wars "Barbary Wars"). The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") saw the [blockade](/wiki/Union_blockade "Union blockade") the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") and seizing control of its rivers. In [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the Navy was central in defeating the [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy") in the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") and *[Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine")* [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") in the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic"), supplanting the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") as the world's largest navy. During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the Navy, rivalled by the [Soviet Navy](/wiki/Soviet_Navy "Soviet Navy"), began providing [nuclear deterrence](/wiki/Nuclear_deterrence "Nuclear deterrence") via [its ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/41_for_Freedom "41 for Freedom"). The Navy was a major participant in the [Korean](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam Wars](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and later the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars") and [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"). A global [power projecting](/wiki/Power_projection "Power projection") [blue-water navy](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy"), current large deployments include the [Western Pacific](/wiki/United_States_Indo-Pacific_Command "United States Indo-Pacific Command"), the [Mediterranean](/wiki/United_States_European_Command "United States European Command"), and the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/United_States_Central_Command "United States Central Command"), and it can and rapidly respond to regional crises, making it a frequent actor in US [foreign](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_United_States "Foreign policy of the United States") and military policy.

The Navy is part of the [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy"), alongside the coequal [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"), and which is headed by the [secretary of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Navy "United States Secretary of the Navy"). The [chief of naval operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations") (CNO) is the most senior Navy officer serving in the Department of the Navy.[[10]](#cite_note-10) With 336,978 personnel on [active duty](/wiki/Active_duty "Active duty") and 101,583 in the [Ready Reserve](/wiki/Ready_Reserve "Ready Reserve"), the Navy is the third largest of US military service branches. The Navy maintains 290 deployable combat vessels as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit),[[8]](#cite_note-:2-8)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] including the world's largest fleet of [nuclear-powered ships](/wiki/Nuclear_marine_propulsion "Nuclear marine propulsion"), between 11 aircraft carriers and 70 submarines.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The world's largest [naval aviation](/wiki/Naval_aviation "Naval aviation") force with 4,012 operational aircraft,[[12]](#cite_note-12) combat fleets include 471 [F/A-18E/F Super Hornets](/wiki/Boeing_F/A-18E/F_Super_Hornet "Boeing F/A-18E/F Super Hornet") and 458 [Sikorsky MH-60 Seahawk](/wiki/Sikorsky_SH-60_Seahawk "Sikorsky SH-60 Seahawk") helicopters.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Navy operates the [*Ohio*-class submarines](/wiki/Ohio-class_submarine "Ohio-class submarine"), which are assigned 1,895 [nuclear warheads](/wiki/Nuclear_warheads "Nuclear warheads"), representing 51% of the total [US nuclear stockpile](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_States "Nuclear weapons of the United States").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Mission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mission")]

> To recruit, train, equip, and organize to deliver combat ready Naval forces to win conflicts and wars while maintaining security and deterrence through sustained forward presence.

— Mission statement of the United States Navy.[[15]](#cite_note-MissionOfTheNavy-15)

The Navy's three primary areas of responsibility are:[[16]](#cite_note-16)

* The preparation of naval forces necessary for the effective prosecution of war.
* The maintenance of naval aviation, including land-based naval aviation, air transport essential for naval operations, and all air weapons and air techniques involved in the operations and activities of the Navy.
* The development of aircraft, weapons, [military tactics](/wiki/Military_tactics "Military tactics"), technique, organization, and equipment of [naval combat](/wiki/Naval_combat "Naval combat") and service elements.

U.S. Navy training manuals state that the overall mission of the armed forces is "to be prepared to conduct prompt and sustained combat operations in support of the national interest." The Navy's five enduring functions are: [sea control](/wiki/Sea_control "Sea control"), [power projection](/wiki/Power_projection "Power projection"), [deterrence](/wiki/Deterrence_theory "Deterrence theory"), [maritime security](/wiki/Maritime_security "Maritime security"), and [sealift](/wiki/Sealift "Sealift").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the United States Navy](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Navy "History of the United States Navy")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

> It follows then as certain as that night succeeds the day, that without a decisive naval force we can do nothing definitive, and with it, everything honorable and glorious.

— [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") 15 November 1781, to [Marquis de Lafayette](/wiki/Marquis_de_Lafayette "Marquis de Lafayette")[[18]](#cite_note-FamousNavyQuotes-18)

> Would to Heaven we had a navy able to reform those enemies to mankind or crush them into non-existence.

— George Washington 15 August 1786, to Marquis de Lafayette[[19]](#cite_note-Washington_to_Lafayette-19)

> Naval power . . . is the natural defense of the United States.

— [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams")[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The Navy was rooted in the colonial seafaring tradition, which produced a large community of sailors, captains, and shipbuilders.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the early stages of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") had its own [Massachusetts Naval Militia](/wiki/Massachusetts_Naval_Militia "Massachusetts Naval Militia"). The rationale for establishing a national navy was debated in the [Second](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress"). Supporters argued that a navy would protect shipping, defend the coast, and make it easier to seek support from foreign countries. Detractors countered that challenging the British [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), then the world's preeminent naval power, was a foolish undertaking. Commander in Chief [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") resolved the debate when he commissioned the ocean-going [schooner](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner") [USS *Hannah*](/wiki/USS_Hannah "USS Hannah") to interdict British merchantmen and reported the captures to the Congress. On 13 October 1775, the Continental Congress authorized the purchase of two vessels to be armed for a cruise against British merchantmen; this resolution created the [Continental Navy](/wiki/Continental_Navy "Continental Navy") and is considered the first establishment of the U.S. Navy.[[22]](#cite_note-Continental-22) The Continental Navy achieved mixed results; it was successful in a number of engagements and raided many British merchant vessels, but it lost twenty-four of its vessels[[23]](#cite_note-love-23) and at one point was reduced to two in active service.[[24]](#cite_note-Howarth-24) In August 1785, after the Revolutionary War had drawn to a close, [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation") had sold [*Alliance*](/wiki/USS_Alliance_(1778) "USS Alliance (1778)"), the last ship remaining in the Continental Navy due to a lack of funds to maintain the ship or support a navy.[[25]](#cite_note-Alliance-25)[[26]](#cite_note-AbbotVIPICXV-26)

In 1972, the chief of naval operations, Admiral [Elmo Zumwalt](/wiki/Elmo_Zumwalt "Elmo Zumwalt"), authorized the Navy to celebrate its birthday on 13 October to honor the establishment of the Continental Navy in 1775.[[22]](#cite_note-Continental-22)[[27]](#cite_note-New_Navy_Birthday-27)

### From re-establishment to the Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: From re-establishment to the Civil War")]

See also: [Union Navy](/wiki/Union_Navy "Union Navy")

The United States was without a navy for nearly a decade, a situation that exposed U.S. merchant ships to attacks by the [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates"). The sole armed maritime presence between 1790 and the launching of the U.S. Navy's first warships in 1797 was the [U.S. Revenue-Marine](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service"), the primary predecessor of the [U.S. Coast Guard](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard "U.S. Coast Guard"). Although the United States Revenue Cutter Service conducted operations against the pirates, the pirates' depredations far outstripped its abilities and Congress passed the [Naval Act of 1794](/wiki/Naval_Act_of_1794 "Naval Act of 1794") that established a permanent standing navy on 27 March 1794.[[28]](#cite_note-US-28) The Naval Act ordered the construction and manning of [six frigates](/wiki/Original_six_frigates_of_the_United_States_Navy "Original six frigates of the United States Navy") and, by October 1797,[[23]](#cite_note-love-23) the first three were brought into service: [USS *United States*](/wiki/USS_United_States_(1797) "USS United States (1797)"), [USS *Constellation*](/wiki/USS_Constellation_(1797) "USS Constellation (1797)"), and [USS *Constitution*](/wiki/USS_Constitution "USS Constitution"). Due to his strong posture on having a strong standing Navy during this period, [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") is "often called the father of the American Navy".[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 1798–99 the Navy was involved in an undeclared [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War") with France.[[31]](#cite_note-31) From 1801 to 1805, in the [First Barbary War](/wiki/First_Barbary_War "First Barbary War"), the U.S. Navy defended U.S. ships from the Barbary pirates, blockaded the Barbary ports and executed attacks against the Barbary' fleets.

The U.S. Navy saw substantial action in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), where it fought numerous engagements with Royal Navy. It emerged victorious in the [Battle of Lake Erie](/wiki/Battle_of_Lake_Erie "Battle of Lake Erie") and prevented the region from becoming a threat to American operations in the area. The result was a major victory for the U.S. Army at the [Niagara Frontier](/wiki/War_of_1812#Niagara_frontier,_1813 "War of 1812") of the war, and the defeat of [Tecumseh's confederacy](/wiki/Tecumseh%27s_confederacy "Tecumseh's confederacy") at the [Battle of the Thames](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Thames "Battle of the Thames"). Despite this, the U.S. Navy could not prevent the British from blockading its ports and landing troops.[[32]](#cite_note-multiple1-32) But after the War of 1812 ended in 1815, the U.S. Navy primarily focused its attention on protecting American shipping assets, sending squadrons to the Caribbean, the Mediterranean, where it participated in the [Second Barbary War](/wiki/Second_Barbary_War "Second Barbary War") that ended piracy in the region, South America, Africa, and the Pacific.[[23]](#cite_note-love-23) From 1819 to the outbreak of the Civil War, the [Africa Squadron](/wiki/Africa_Squadron "Africa Squadron") operated to suppress the [slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade"), seizing 36 slave ships, although its contribution was smaller than that of the much larger Royal Navy. After 1840 several [secretaries of the navy](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Navy "United States Secretary of the Navy") were southerners who advocated for strengthening southern naval defenses, expanding the fleet, and making naval technological improvements.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Thomas_Birch_-_Engagement_Between_the_%22Constitution%22_and_the_%22Guerri%C3%A8re%22.jpg/250px-Thomas_Birch_-_Engagement_Between_the_%22Constitution%22_and_the_%22Guerri%C3%A8re%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Birch_-_Engagement_Between_the_%22Constitution%22_and_the_%22Guerri%C3%A8re%22.jpg)

[USS *Constitution* vs HMS *Guerriere*](/wiki/USS_Constitution_vs_HMS_Guerriere "USS Constitution vs HMS Guerriere") during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")

During the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") the U.S. Navy blockaded Mexican ports, capturing or burning the Mexican fleet in the [Gulf of California](/wiki/Gulf_of_California "Gulf of California") and capturing all major cities in [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") peninsula. In 1846–1848 the Navy successfully used the [Pacific Squadron](/wiki/Pacific_Squadron "Pacific Squadron") under Commodore [Robert F. Stockton](/wiki/Robert_F._Stockton "Robert F. Stockton") and its marines and blue-jackets to facilitate the capture of California with large-scale land operations coordinated with the local militia organized in the [California Battalion](/wiki/California_Battalion "California Battalion"). The Navy conducted the U.S. military's first large-scale amphibious joint operation by successfully landing 12,000 army troops with their equipment in one day at [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz_(city) "Veracruz (city)"), Mexico. When larger guns were needed to bombard Veracruz, Navy volunteers landed large guns and manned them in the successful bombardment and capture of the city. This successful landing and capture of Veracruz opened the way for the capture of Mexico City and the end of the war.[[32]](#cite_note-multiple1-32) The U.S. Navy established itself as a player in [United States foreign policy](/wiki/United_States_foreign_policy "United States foreign policy") through the actions of [Commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(United_States) "Commodore (United States)") [Matthew C. Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry") in Japan, which resulted in the [Convention of Kanagawa](/wiki/Convention_of_Kanagawa "Convention of Kanagawa") in 1854.

Naval power played a significant role during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), in which the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") had a distinct advantage over the [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") on the seas.[[32]](#cite_note-multiple1-32) A [Union blockade](/wiki/Union_blockade "Union blockade") on all major ports shut down exports and the coastal trade, but blockade runners provided a thin lifeline. The [Brown-water navy](/wiki/Brown-water_navy "Brown-water navy") components of the U.S. navy control of the river systems made internal travel difficult for Confederates and easy for the Union. The war saw [ironclad warships](/wiki/Ironclad_warship "Ironclad warship") in combat for the first time at the [Battle of Hampton Roads](/wiki/Battle_of_Hampton_Roads "Battle of Hampton Roads") in 1862, which pitted [USS *Monitor*](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor") against [CSS *Virginia*](/wiki/CSS_Virginia "CSS Virginia").[[34]](#cite_note-34) For two decades after the war, however, the U.S. Navy's fleet was neglected and became [technologically obsolete](/wiki/Technologically_obsolete "Technologically obsolete").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Us-atlantic-fleet-1907.jpg/250px-Us-atlantic-fleet-1907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Us-atlantic-fleet-1907.jpg)

The [Great White Fleet](/wiki/Great_White_Fleet "Great White Fleet") demonstrating U.S. naval power in 1907; it was proof that the U.S. Navy had [blue-water capability](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy").

A modernization program beginning in the 1880s when the first steel-hulled warships stimulated the American steel industry, and "the new steel navy" was born.[[36]](#cite_note-36) This rapid expansion of the U.S. Navy and its decisive victory over the outdated [Spanish Navy](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy") in 1898 brought a new respect for American technical quality. Rapid building of at first pre-dreadnoughts, then [dreadnoughts](/wiki/Dreadnoughts "Dreadnoughts") brought the U.S. in line with the navies of countries such as Britain and Germany. In 1907, most of the Navy's battleships, with several support vessels, dubbed the [Great White Fleet](/wiki/Great_White_Fleet "Great White Fleet"), were showcased in a 14-month circumnavigation of the world. Ordered by President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), it was a mission designed to demonstrate the Navy's capability to extend to the global theater.[[23]](#cite_note-love-23) By 1911, the U.S. had begun building the super-dreadnoughts at a pace to eventually become competitive with Britain.[[37]](#cite_note-37) 1911 also saw the first naval aircraft with the navy[[38]](#cite_note-38) which would lead to the informal establishment of **United States Naval Flying Corps** to protect shore bases. It was not until 1921 [US naval aviation](/wiki/Bureau_of_Aeronautics "Bureau of Aeronautics") truly commenced.

#### World War I and interwar years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World War I and interwar years")]

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the U.S. Navy spent much of its resources protecting and shipping hundreds of thousands of soldiers and marines of the [American Expeditionary Force](/wiki/American_Expeditionary_Force "American Expeditionary Force") and war supplies across the Atlantic in [U-boat](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") infested waters with the [Cruiser and Transport Force](/wiki/Cruiser_and_Transport_Force "Cruiser and Transport Force"). It also concentrated on laying the [North Sea Mine Barrage](/wiki/North_Sea_Mine_Barrage "North Sea Mine Barrage"). Hesitation by the senior command meant that naval forces were not contributed until late 1917. [Battleship Division Nine](/wiki/United_States_Battleship_Division_Nine_(World_War_I) "United States Battleship Division Nine (World War I)") was dispatched to Britain and served as the Sixth Battle Squadron of the British Grand Fleet. Its presence allowed the British to decommission some older ships and reuse the crews on smaller vessels. Destroyers and U.S. Naval Air Force units like the [Northern Bombing Group](/wiki/Northern_Bombing_Group "Northern Bombing Group") contributed to the anti-submarine operations. The strength of the United States Navy grew under an ambitious ship building program associated with the [Naval Act of 1916](/wiki/Naval_Act_of_1916 "Naval Act of 1916").

Naval construction, especially of battleships, was limited by the [Washington Naval Conference](/wiki/Washington_Naval_Conference "Washington Naval Conference") of 1921–22, the first arms control conference in history. The aircraft carriers [USS *Saratoga* (CV-3)](/wiki/USS_Saratoga_(CV-3) "USS Saratoga (CV-3)") and [USS *Lexington* (CV-2)](/wiki/USS_Lexington_(CV-2) "USS Lexington (CV-2)") were built on the hulls of partially built battle cruisers that had been canceled by the treaty. The [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") used [Public Works Administration](/wiki/Public_Works_Administration "Public Works Administration") funds to build warships, such as [USS *Yorktown* (CV-5)](/wiki/USS_Yorktown_(CV-5) "USS Yorktown (CV-5)") and [USS *Enterprise* (CV-6)](/wiki/USS_Enterprise_(CV-6) "USS Enterprise (CV-6)"). By 1936, with the completion of [USS *Wasp* (CV-7)](/wiki/USS_Wasp_(CV-7) "USS Wasp (CV-7)"), the U.S. Navy possessed a carrier fleet of 165,000 tonnes [displacement](/wiki/Displacement_(ship) "Displacement (ship)"), although this figure was nominally recorded as 135,000 tonnes to comply with treaty limitations. [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Roosevelt "Franklin Roosevelt"), the number two official in the Navy Department during World War I, appreciated the Navy and gave it strong support. In return, senior leaders were eager for innovation and experimented with new technologies, such as magnetic torpedoes, and developed a strategy called [War Plan Orange](/wiki/War_Plan_Orange "War Plan Orange") for victory in the Pacific in a hypothetical war with Japan that would eventually become reality.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: World War II")]

Main articles: [United States Navy in World War II](/wiki/United_States_Navy_in_World_War_II "United States Navy in World War II") and [Naval history of World War II](/wiki/Naval_history_of_World_War_II "Naval history of World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/New_Mexico_class_battleship_bombarding_Okinawa.jpg/250px-New_Mexico_class_battleship_bombarding_Okinawa.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Mexico_class_battleship_bombarding_Okinawa.jpg)

Battleship [USS *Idaho*](/wiki/USS_Idaho_(BB-42) "USS Idaho (BB-42)") [shelling Okinawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa "Battle of Okinawa") on 1 April 1945

The U.S. Navy grew into a formidable force in the years prior to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), with battleship production being restarted in 1937, commencing with [USS *North Carolina* (BB-55)](/wiki/USS_North_Carolina_(BB-55) "USS North Carolina (BB-55)"). Though ultimately unsuccessful, Japan tried to neutralize this strategic threat with the surprise [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") on 7 December 1941. Following [American entry into the war](/wiki/Consequences_of_the_attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Consequences of the attack on Pearl Harbor"), the U.S. Navy grew tremendously as the United States was faced with a two-front war on the seas. It achieved notable acclaim in the [Pacific Theater](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), where it was instrumental to the Allies' successful "[island hopping](/wiki/Leapfrogging_(strategy) "Leapfrogging (strategy)")" campaign.[[24]](#cite_note-Howarth-24) The U.S. Navy participated in many significant battles, including the [Battle of the Coral Sea](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Coral_Sea "Battle of the Coral Sea"), the [Battle of Midway](/wiki/Battle_of_Midway "Battle of Midway"), the [Solomon Islands Campaign](/wiki/Solomon_Islands_Campaign "Solomon Islands Campaign"), the [Battle of the Philippine Sea](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Philippine_Sea "Battle of the Philippine Sea"), the [Battle of Leyte Gulf](/wiki/Battle_of_Leyte_Gulf "Battle of Leyte Gulf"), and the [Battle of Okinawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Okinawa "Battle of Okinawa"). By 1943, the navy's size was larger than the combined fleets of all the other combatant nations in World War II.[[40]](#cite_note-Tread-40) By war's end in 1945, the U.S. Navy had added hundreds of new ships, including 18 aircraft carriers and 8 battleships, and had over 70% of the world's total numbers and total tonnage of naval vessels of 1,000 tons or greater.[[41]](#cite_note-Defense_Analysis;_Dec_2001,_Vol._17_Issue_3,_p._259–265-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) At its peak, the U.S. Navy was operating 6,768 ships on [V-J Day](/wiki/V-J_Day "V-J Day") in August 1945.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/US_Navy_Issue%2C_3c%2C_1945.jpg/250px-US_Navy_Issue%2C_3c%2C_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_Issue,_3c,_1945.jpg)

On [Navy Day](/wiki/Navy_Day#United_States "Navy Day"), 27 October 1945, the U.S. Post Office issued a commemorative stamp in honor of the Navy and the end of WW2.

Doctrine had significantly shifted by the end of the war. The U.S. Navy had followed in the footsteps of the navies of Great Britain and Germany which favored concentrated groups of battleships as their main offensive naval weapons.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The development of the aircraft carrier and its devastating use by the Japanese against the U.S. at Pearl Harbor, however, shifted U.S. thinking. The Pearl Harbor attack destroyed or took out of action a significant number of U.S. Navy battleships. This placed much of the burden of retaliating against the Japanese on the small number of aircraft carriers.[[45]](#cite_note-45) During World War II some 4,000,000 Americans served in the United States Navy.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

#### Cold War and 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cold War and 1990s")]

See also: [1989 United States Navy order of battle](/wiki/1989_United_States_Navy_order_of_battle "1989 United States Navy order of battle") and [American naval ballistic systems](/wiki/American_naval_ballistic_systems "American naval ballistic systems")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/USS_George_Washington_%28SSBN-598%29_underway_at_sea%2C_circa_in_the_1970s.jpg/250px-USS_George_Washington_%28SSBN-598%29_underway_at_sea%2C_circa_in_the_1970s.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_George_Washington_(SSBN-598)_underway_at_sea,_circa_in_the_1970s.jpg)

[USS *George Washington*](/wiki/USS_George_Washington_(SSBN-598) "USS George Washington (SSBN-598)"), a ballistic missile submarine

The potential for armed conflict with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") pushed the U.S. Navy to continue its technological advancement by developing new weapons systems, ships, and aircraft. U.S. naval strategy changed to that of forward deployment in support of U.S. allies with an emphasis on carrier battle groups.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The navy was a major participant in the [Korean](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam Wars](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), blockaded Cuba during the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis"), and, through the use of [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarines "Ballistic missile submarines"), became an important aspect of the United States' [nuclear strategic deterrence](/wiki/Mutual_assured_destruction "Mutual assured destruction") policy. The U.S. Navy conducted various combat operations in the Persian Gulf against Iran in 1987 and 1988, most notably [Operation Praying Mantis](/wiki/Operation_Praying_Mantis "Operation Praying Mantis"). The Navy was extensively involved in [Operation Urgent Fury](/wiki/Operation_Urgent_Fury "Operation Urgent Fury"), [Operation Desert Shield](/wiki/Operation_Desert_Shield_(Gulf_War) "Operation Desert Shield (Gulf War)"), [Operation Desert Storm](/wiki/Operation_Desert_Storm "Operation Desert Storm"), [Operation Deliberate Force](/wiki/Operation_Deliberate_Force "Operation Deliberate Force"), [Operation Allied Force](/wiki/Operation_Allied_Force "Operation Allied Force"), [Operation Desert Fox](/wiki/Operation_Desert_Fox "Operation Desert Fox") and [Operation Southern Watch](/wiki/Operation_Southern_Watch "Operation Southern Watch").

The U.S. Navy has also been involved in search and rescue/search and salvage operations, sometimes in conjunction with vessels of other countries as well as with U.S. Coast Guard ships. Two examples are the [1966 Palomares B-52 crash](/wiki/1966_Palomares_B-52_crash "1966 Palomares B-52 crash") incident and the subsequent search for missing hydrogen bombs, and Task Force 71 of the Seventh Fleet's operation in search for [Korean Air Lines Flight 007](/wiki/Korean_Air_Lines_Flight_007 "Korean Air Lines Flight 007"), shot down by the Soviets on 1 September 1983.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/US_Navy_100913-N-4973M-012_Lt._j.g._Craig_Mueller%2C_from_St._Louis%2C_Mo.%2C_and_Lt._j.g._Zach_Decker%2C_from_Boulder%2C_Co.%2C_monitor_the_defense_systems_ab.jpg/250px-US_Navy_100913-N-4973M-012_Lt._j.g._Craig_Mueller%2C_from_St._Louis%2C_Mo.%2C_and_Lt._j.g._Zach_Decker%2C_from_Boulder%2C_Co.%2C_monitor_the_defense_systems_ab.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_100913-N-4973M-012_Lt._j.g._Craig_Mueller,_from_St._Louis,_Mo.,_and_Lt._j.g._Zach_Decker,_from_Boulder,_Co.,_monitor_the_defense_systems_ab.jpg)

U.S. Navy officers aboard the aircraft carrier [USS *Abraham Lincoln*](/wiki/USS_Abraham_Lincoln_(CVN-72) "USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72)") monitor defense systems during early 2010s [maritime security operations](/wiki/Maritime_security_operations "Maritime security operations") exercises.

The U.S. Navy continues to be a major support to U.S. interests in the 21st century. Since the end of the Cold War, it has shifted its focus from preparations for large-scale war with the Soviet Union to special operations and strike missions in regional conflicts.[[48]](#cite_note-forward-48) The navy participated in [Operation Enduring Freedom](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), [Operation Iraqi Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Iraqi_Freedom "Operation Iraqi Freedom"), and is a major participant in the ongoing [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"), largely in this capacity. Development continues on new ships and weapons, including the [*Gerald R. Ford*-class](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford-class_aircraft_carrier "Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier") aircraft carrier and the [Littoral combat ship](/wiki/Littoral_combat_ship "Littoral combat ship"). Because of its size, weapons technology, and ability to project force far from U.S. shores, the current U.S. Navy remains an asset for the United States. Moreover, it is the principal means through which the U.S. maintains international global order, namely by safeguarding global trade and protecting allied nations.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In 2007, the U.S. Navy joined with the [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/U.S._Marine_Corps "U.S. Marine Corps") and U.S. Coast Guard to adopt a new maritime strategy called [A Cooperative Strategy for 21st Century Seapower](/wiki/A_Cooperative_Strategy_for_21st_Century_Seapower "A Cooperative Strategy for 21st Century Seapower") that raises the notion of prevention of war to the same philosophical level as the conduct of war. The strategy was presented by the [Chief of Naval Operations](/wiki/Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Chief of Naval Operations"), the [Commandant of the Marine Corps](/wiki/Commandant_of_the_United_States_Marine_Corps "Commandant of the United States Marine Corps"), and [Commandant of the Coast Guard](/wiki/Commandant_of_the_Coast_Guard "Commandant of the Coast Guard") at the International Sea Power Symposium in [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island") on 17 October 2007.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The strategy recognized the economic links of the global system and how any disruption due to regional crises (man-made or natural) can adversely impact the U.S. economy and quality of life. This new strategy charts a course for the Navy, Coast Guard, and Marine Corps to work collectively with each other and international partners to prevent these crises from occurring or reacting quickly should one occur to prevent negative impacts on the U.S.

In 2010, Admiral Gary Roughead, Chief of Naval Operations, noted that demands on the Navy have grown as the fleet has shrunk and that in the face of declining budgets in the future, the U.S. Navy must rely even more on international partnerships.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In its 2013 budget request, the navy focused on retaining all eleven big deck carriers, at the expense of cutting numbers of smaller ships and delaying the SSBN replacement.[[52]](#cite_note-52) By the next year the USN found itself unable to maintain eleven aircraft carriers in the face of the expiration of budget relief offered by the [Bipartisan Budget Act of 2013](/wiki/Bipartisan_Budget_Act_of_2013 "Bipartisan Budget Act of 2013") and CNO [Jonathan Greenert](/wiki/Jonathan_Greenert "Jonathan Greenert") said that a ten ship carrier fleet would not be able to sustainably support military requirements.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The British [First Sea Lord](/wiki/First_Sea_Lord "First Sea Lord") [George Zambellas](/wiki/George_Zambellas "George Zambellas") said that[[54]](#cite_note-thediplomat1-54) the USN had switched from "outcome-led to resource-led" planning.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

One significant change in U.S. policymaking that is having a major effect on naval planning is the [Pivot to East Asia](/wiki/East_Asian_foreign_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "East Asian foreign policy of the Obama administration"). In response, the [Secretary of the Navy](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Navy "Secretary of the Navy") [Ray Mabus](/wiki/Ray_Mabus "Ray Mabus") stated in 2015 that 60 percent of the total U.S. fleet will be deployed to the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") by 2020.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The Navy's most recent 30-year shipbuilding plan, published in 2016, calls for a future fleet of 350 ships to meet the challenges of an increasingly competitive international environment.[[54]](#cite_note-thediplomat1-54) A provision of the 2018 [National Defense Authorization Act](/wiki/National_Defense_Authorization_Act_for_Fiscal_Year_2018 "National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2018") called for expanding the naval fleet to 355 ships "as soon as practicable", but did not establish additional funding nor a timeline.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Organization")]

Main article: [Structure of the United States Navy](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Navy "Structure of the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg/250px-Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg)

Organization of the United States Navy within the Department of Defense

The U.S. Navy falls under the administration of the [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy"), under civilian leadership of the Secretary of the Navy (SECNAV). The most senior naval officer is the Chief of Naval Operations (CNO), a four-star admiral who is immediately under and reports to the Secretary of the Navy. At the same time, the Chief of Naval Operations is a member of the [Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Joint Chiefs of Staff") (JCS), though the JCS plays only an advisory role to the President and does not nominally form part of the [chain of command](/wiki/Command_hierarchy "Command hierarchy"). The Secretary of the Navy and Chief of Naval Operations are responsible for organizing, recruiting, training, and equipping the Navy so that it is ready for operation under the commanders of the [unified combatant commands](/wiki/Unified_combatant_command "Unified combatant command").

### Operating forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Operating forces")]

Main article: [List of units of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_units_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of units of the United States Navy")

Further information: [Structure of the United States Navy § Numbered fleets](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Navy#Numbered_fleets "Structure of the United States Navy")

[![Areas of responsibility for each of the United States Navy Fleets.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/USN_Fleets_%282009%29.png/250px-USN_Fleets_%282009%29.png)](/wiki/File:USN_Fleets_(2009).png)

Areas of responsibility for each of the United States Navy fleets. [Tenth Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Tenth_Fleet "United States Tenth Fleet") serves as the numbered fleet for [U.S. Fleet Cyber Command](/wiki/U.S._Fleet_Cyber_Command "U.S. Fleet Cyber Command") and therefore is not shown.

There are ten components in the operating forces of the U.S. Navy:[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

* The [United States Fleet Forces Command](/wiki/United_States_Fleet_Forces_Command "United States Fleet Forces Command")
* The [United States Pacific Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Pacific_Fleet "United States Pacific Fleet")
* The [United States Naval Forces Central Command](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Forces_Central_Command "United States Naval Forces Central Command")
* The [United States Naval Forces Europe and Africa](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Forces_Europe_and_Africa "United States Naval Forces Europe and Africa")
* The [United States Naval Forces Southern Command](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Forces_Southern_Command "United States Naval Forces Southern Command")
* The [Naval Network Warfare Command](/wiki/Naval_Network_Warfare_Command "Naval Network Warfare Command")
* The [Navy Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve")
* The [United States Naval Special Warfare Command](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Special_Warfare_Command "United States Naval Special Warfare Command")
* The [Operational Test and Evaluation Force](/wiki/Operational_Test_and_Evaluation_Force "Operational Test and Evaluation Force")
* The [Military Sealift Command](/wiki/Military_Sealift_Command "Military Sealift Command")

Fleet Forces Command controls a number of unique capabilities, including the [Naval Expeditionary Combat Command](/wiki/Naval_Expeditionary_Combat_Command "Naval Expeditionary Combat Command") and the [Naval Information Forces](/wiki/U.S._Naval_Information_Forces "U.S. Naval Information Forces").

The United States Navy has seven active numbered fleets – [Second](/wiki/United_States_Second_Fleet "United States Second Fleet"), [Third](/wiki/United_States_Third_Fleet "United States Third Fleet"), [Fifth](/wiki/United_States_Fifth_Fleet "United States Fifth Fleet"), [Sixth](/wiki/United_States_Sixth_Fleet "United States Sixth Fleet"), [Seventh](/wiki/United_States_Seventh_Fleet "United States Seventh Fleet") and [Tenth Fleets](/wiki/United_States_Tenth_Fleet "United States Tenth Fleet") are each led by a [vice admiral](/wiki/Vice_admiral_(United_States) "Vice admiral (United States)"), and the [Fourth Fleet](/wiki/United_States_Fourth_Fleet "United States Fourth Fleet") is led by a [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States) "Rear admiral (United States)"). These seven fleets are further grouped under Fleet Forces Command (the former Atlantic Fleet), Pacific Fleet, Naval Forces Europe-Africa, and Naval Forces Central Command, whose commander also doubles as Commander Fifth Fleet; the first three commands being led by four-star admirals. The [United States First Fleet](/wiki/United_States_First_Fleet "United States First Fleet") existed after World War II from 1947, but it was redesignated the Third Fleet in early 1973. The Second Fleet was deactivated in September 2011 but reestablished in August 2018 amid heightened tensions with Russia.[[60]](#cite_note-60) It is headquartered in Norfolk, Virginia, with responsibility over the East Coast and North Atlantic.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In early 2008, the Navy reactivated the Fourth Fleet to control operations in the area controlled by Southern Command, which consists of US assets in and around Central and South America.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Other number fleets were activated during World War II and later deactivated, renumbered, or merged.

### Shore establishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Shore establishments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/USS_Kitty_Hawk_at_Yokosuka.jpg/250px-USS_Kitty_Hawk_at_Yokosuka.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Kitty_Hawk_at_Yokosuka.jpg)

Shore establishments exist to support the mission of the fleet through the use of facilities on land. Command](/wiki/Navy_Space_Command "Navy Space Command"), the [Navy Installations Command](/wiki/Commander,_Navy_Installations_Command "Commander, Navy Installations Command"), the [Naval Meteorology and Oceanography Command](/wiki/Naval_Meteorology_and_Oceanography_Command "Naval Meteorology and Oceanography Command"), the [Naval Information Warfare Systems Command](/wiki/Naval_Information_Warfare_Systems_Command "Naval Information Warfare Systems Command"), the [Naval Facilities Engineering Command](/wiki/Naval_Facilities_Engineering_Command "Naval Facilities Engineering Command"), the [Naval Supply Systems Command](/wiki/Naval_Supply_Systems_Command "Naval Supply Systems Command"), the [Naval Air Systems Command](/wiki/Naval_Air_Systems_Command "Naval Air Systems Command"), the [Naval Sea Systems Command](/wiki/Naval_Sea_Systems_Command "Naval Sea Systems Command"), the [Bureau of Medicine and Surgery](/wiki/Bureau_of_Medicine_and_Surgery "Bureau of Medicine and Surgery"), the [Bureau of Naval Personnel](/wiki/Bureau_of_Naval_Personnel "Bureau of Naval Personnel"), the [Office of Naval Research](/wiki/Office_of_Naval_Research "Office of Naval Research"), the [Office of Naval Intelligence](/wiki/Office_of_Naval_Intelligence "Office of Naval Intelligence"), the [United States Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy"), the [Naval Safety Command](/wiki/Naval_Safety_Command "Naval Safety Command"), the [Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center](/wiki/Naval_Aviation_Warfighting_Development_Center "Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center"), and the [United States Naval Observatory](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Observatory "United States Naval Observatory").[[63]](#cite_note-63) Official Navy websites list the [Office of the Chief of Naval Operations](/wiki/Office_of_the_Chief_of_Naval_Operations "Office of the Chief of Naval Operations") and the Chief of Naval Operations as part of the shore establishment, but these two entities effectively sit superior to the other organizations, playing a coordinating role.[[64]](#cite_note-:0-64)

### Relationships with other service branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Relationships with other service branches")]

#### United States Marine Corps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United States Marine Corps")]

Main article: [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/F-18A_Hornet_VMFA-451_USS_Coral_Sea_1989.jpeg/250px-F-18A_Hornet_VMFA-451_USS_Coral_Sea_1989.jpeg)](/wiki/File:F-18A_Hornet_VMFA-451_USS_Coral_Sea_1989.jpeg)

A [Marine](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") [F/A-18](/wiki/F/A-18 "F/A-18") from [VMFA-451](/wiki/VMFA-451 "VMFA-451") preparing to launch from [USS *Coral Sea*](/wiki/USS_Coral_Sea_(CV-43) "USS Coral Sea (CV-43)")

In 1834, the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") came under the Department of the Navy.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Historically, the Navy has had a unique relationship with the USMC, partly because they both specialize in seaborne operations. Together the Navy and Marine Corps form the Department of the Navy and report to the Secretary of the Navy. However, the Marine Corps is a distinct, separate service branch[[66]](#cite_note-NSA1947sec606-66) with its own uniformed service chief – the Commandant of the Marine Corps, a four-star general.

The Marine Corps depends on the Navy for medical support (dentists, [doctors](/wiki/Medical_doctor "Medical doctor"), nurses, medical technicians known as [corpsmen](/wiki/Hospital_corpsman "Hospital corpsman")) and religious support (chaplains). Thus, Navy officers and enlisted sailors fulfill these roles. When attached to Marine Corps units deployed to an operational environment they generally wear Marine camouflage uniforms, but otherwise, they wear Navy [dress uniforms](/wiki/Dress_uniform "Dress uniform") unless they opt to conform to Marine Corps grooming standards.[[64]](#cite_note-:0-64)

In the operational environment, as an expeditionary force specializing in amphibious operations, Marines often embark on Navy ships to conduct operations from beyond territorial waters. Marine units deploying as part of a Marine Air-Ground Task Force (MAGTF) operate under the command of the existing Marine chain of command. Although Marine units routinely operate from amphibious assault ships, the relationship has evolved over the years much as the Commander of the Carrier Air Group/Wing (CAG) does not work for the carrier commanding officer, but coordinates with the ship's CO and staff. Some Marine aviation squadrons, usually fixed-wing assigned to carrier air wings train and operate alongside Navy squadrons; they fly similar missions and often fly sorties together under the cognizance of the CAG. Aviation is where the Navy and Marines share the most common ground since aircrews are guided in their use of aircraft by standard procedures outlined in a series of publications known as [NATOPS](/wiki/NATOPS "NATOPS") manuals.

#### United States Coast Guard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: United States Coast Guard")]

Main article: [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Flickr_-_Official_U.S._Navy_Imagery_-_U.S._Coast_Guard_helicopters_land_aboard_USS_Wasp..jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Official_U.S._Navy_Imagery_-_U.S._Coast_Guard_helicopters_land_aboard_USS_Wasp..jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Official_U.S._Navy_Imagery_-_U.S._Coast_Guard_helicopters_land_aboard_USS_Wasp..jpg)

A U.S. Coast Guard helicopter preparing to land on the flight deck of the amphibious assault ship [USS *Wasp*](/wiki/USS_Wasp_(LHD-1) "USS Wasp (LHD-1)")

The [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard"), in its peacetime role with the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/Department_of_Homeland_Security "Department of Homeland Security"), fulfills its law enforcement and rescue role in the maritime environment. It provides [Law Enforcement Detachments](/wiki/Law_Enforcement_Detachments "Law Enforcement Detachments") (LEDETs) to Navy vessels, where they perform arrests and other law enforcement duties during naval boarding and interdiction missions. In times of war, the Coast Guard may be called upon to operate as a service within the Navy.[[67]](#cite_note-67) At other times, Coast Guard [Port Security Units](/wiki/Port_Security_Unit "Port Security Unit") are sent overseas to guard the security of ports and other assets. The Coast Guard also jointly staffs the Navy's naval coastal warfare groups and squadrons (the latter of which were known as harbor defense commands until late-2004), which oversee defense efforts in foreign littoral combat and inshore areas.

## Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Personnel")]

Main article: [Personnel of the United States Navy](/wiki/Personnel_of_the_United_States_Navy "Personnel of the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/US_Navy_SEALs_at_Zhawar_Kili_cave_entrance.jpg/250px-US_Navy_SEALs_at_Zhawar_Kili_cave_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_SEALs_at_Zhawar_Kili_cave_entrance.jpg)

[Navy SEALs](/wiki/United_States_Navy_SEALs "United States Navy SEALs") at one of the entrances to the [Zhawar Kili](/wiki/Zhawar_Kili "Zhawar Kili") cave complex

The United States Navy has over 400,000 personnel, approximately a quarter of whom are in ready reserve. Of those on active duty, more than eighty percent are [enlisted](/wiki/Enlisted_rank "Enlisted rank") sailors and around fifteen percent are [commissioned officers](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer"); the rest are [midshipmen](/wiki/Midshipmen "Midshipmen") of the United States Naval Academy and midshipmen of the [Naval Reserve Officer Training Corps](/wiki/Naval_Reserve_Officer_Training_Corps "Naval Reserve Officer Training Corps") at over 180 universities around the country and officer candidates at the Navy's [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School_(United_States_Navy) "Officer Candidate School (United States Navy)").[[4]](#cite_note-status-4)

Enlisted sailors complete basic military training at [boot camp](/wiki/Recruit_Training_Command,_Great_Lakes,_Illinois "Recruit Training Command, Great Lakes, Illinois") and then are sent to complete training for their individual [careers](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_ratings "List of United States Navy ratings").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Sailors prove they have mastered skills and deserve responsibilities by completing Personnel Qualification Standards (PQS) tasks and examinations. Among the most important is the "warfare qualification", which denotes a journeyman level of capability in Surface Warfare, Aviation Warfare, Information Dominance Warfare, Naval Aircrew, Special Warfare, Seabee Warfare, Submarine Warfare or Expeditionary Warfare. Many qualifications are denoted on a sailor's uniform with [U.S. Navy badges and insignia](/wiki/Badges_of_the_United_States_Navy "Badges of the United States Navy").

### Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

See also: [Uniforms of the United States Navy](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_United_States_Navy "Uniforms of the United States Navy")

The uniforms of the U.S. Navy have evolved gradually since the first uniform regulations for [officers](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)") were issued in 1802 on the formation of the Navy Department. The predominant colors of U.S. Navy uniforms are navy blue and white. U.S. Navy uniforms were based on Royal Navy uniforms of the time and have tended to follow that template.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Warrant and chief warrant officer ranks are held by technical specialists who direct specific activities essential to the proper operation of the ship, which also require commissioned officer authority.[[72]](#cite_note-WO-4-73) Navy warrant officers serve in 30 specialties covering five categories. Warrant officers should not be confused with the [limited duty officer](/wiki/Limited_duty_officer "Limited duty officer") (LDO) in the Navy. Warrant officers perform duties that are directly related to their previous enlisted service and specialized training. This allows the Navy to capitalize on the experience of warrant officers without having to frequently transition them to other duty assignments for advancement.[[73]](#cite_note-WO-5-74) Most Navy warrant officers are accessed from the [chief petty officer](/wiki/Chief_petty_officer_(United_States) "Chief petty officer (United States)") pay grades, E-7 through E-9, analogous to a senior non-commissioned officer in the other services, and must have a minimum 14 years in service.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

#### Enlisted

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Enlisted")]

See also: [List of United States Navy enlisted rates](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_enlisted_rates "List of United States Navy enlisted rates"), [Ranks and insignia of NATO navies enlisted](/wiki/Ranks_and_insignia_of_NATO_navies_enlisted "Ranks and insignia of NATO navies enlisted"), and [List of United States Navy ratings](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_ratings "List of United States Navy ratings")

Sailors in pay grades E-1 through E-3 are considered to be in apprenticeships.[[75]](#cite_note-NAVYDAT2-76) They are divided into five definable groups, with colored group rate marks designating the group to which they belong: Seaman, Fireman, Airman, Constructionman, and Hospitalman. **E-4** to **E-6** are [non-commissioned officers](/wiki/Non-commissioned_officer "Non-commissioned officer") (NCOs), and are specifically called [Petty officers](/wiki/Petty_officer "Petty officer") in the Navy.[[76]](#cite_note-BUPERS-77) Petty Officers perform not only the duties of their specific career field but also serve as leaders to junior enlisted personnel. E-7 to E-9 are still considered Petty Officers, but are considered a separate community within the Navy. They have separate berthing and dining facilities (where feasible), wear separate uniforms, and perform separate duties.

After attaining the rate of Master Chief Petty Officer, a service member may choose to further their career by becoming a [Command Master Chief Petty Officer](/wiki/Command_Master_Chief_Petty_Officer "Command Master Chief Petty Officer") (CMC). A CMC is considered to be the senior-most enlisted service member within a command, and is the special assistant to the [Commanding Officer](/wiki/Commanding_Officer "Commanding Officer") in all matters pertaining to the health, welfare, job satisfaction, morale, use, advancement and training of the command's enlisted personnel.[[77]](#cite_note-MILCMDMC-78)[[78]](#cite_note-IPUBCMC-79) CMCs can be Command level (within a single unit, such as a ship or shore station), Fleet level (squadrons consisting of multiple operational units, headed by a flag officer or commodore), or Force level (consisting of a separate community within the Navy, such as Subsurface, Air, Reserves).[[79]](#cite_note-OPNAV1306-80)

CMC insignia are similar to the insignia for Master Chief, except that the rating symbol is replaced by an inverted five-point star, reflecting a change in their rating from their previous rating (i.e., MMCM) to CMDCM. The stars for Command Master Chief are silver, while stars for Fleet, and gold stars for Force. Additionally, CMCs wear a badge, worn on their left breast pocket, denoting their title (Command/Fleet/Force).[[78]](#cite_note-IPUBCMC-79)[[80]](#cite_note-BUPERSCPO-81)

#### Badges of the United States Navy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Badges of the United States Navy")]

See also: [Badges of the United States Navy](/wiki/Badges_of_the_United_States_Navy "Badges of the United States Navy")

Insignia and badges of the United States Navy are military "badges" issued by the Department of the Navy to naval service members who achieve certain qualifications and accomplishments while serving on both active and reserve duty in the United States Navy. Most naval aviation insignia are also permitted for wear on uniforms of the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/Badges_of_the_United_States_Marine_Corps "Badges of the United States Marine Corps").

As described in Chapter 5 of U.S. Navy Uniform Regulations,[[81]](#cite_note-navy_reg-82) "badges" are categorized as *breast insignia* (usually worn immediately above and below ribbons) and *identification badges* (usually worn at breast pocket level).[[82]](#cite_note-chapter_5-83) Breast insignia are further divided between *command* and *warfare and other qualification*.[[83]](#cite_note-section_2-84)

Insignia come in the form of metal "pin-on devices" worn on formal uniforms and embroidered "tape strips" worn on work uniforms. For the purpose of this article, the general term "insignia" shall be used to describe both, as it is done in Navy Uniform Regulations. The term "badge", although used ambiguously in other military branches and in informal speak to describe any pin, patch, or tab, is exclusive to *identification badges*[[84]](#cite_note-article_5201.2-85) and authorized *[marksmanship awards](/wiki/Distinguished_Shot_Badge "Distinguished Shot Badge")*[[85]](#cite_note-article_5310-86) according to the language in Navy Uniform Regulations, Chapter 5. Below are just a few of the many badges maintained by the Navy. The rest can be seen in the article cited at the top of this section:
