## Bases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Bases")]

Main article: [List of United States Navy installations](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_installations "List of United States Navy installations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/United_States_Navy_bases.svg/500px-United_States_Navy_bases.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_States_Navy_bases.svg)

Map of naval bases in the United States

The size, complexity, and international presence of the United States Navy requires a large number of navy installations to support its operations. While the majority of bases are located inside the United States itself, the Navy maintains a significant number of facilities abroad, either in U.S.-controlled territories or in foreign countries under a [Status of Forces Agreement](/wiki/Status_of_Forces_Agreement "Status of Forces Agreement") (SOFA).

### Eastern United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Eastern United States")]

The second largest concentration of installations is at [Hampton Roads](/wiki/Hampton_Roads "Hampton Roads"), [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), where the navy occupies over 36,000 acres (15,000 ha) of land. Located at Hampton Roads are [Naval Station Norfolk](/wiki/Naval_Station_Norfolk "Naval Station Norfolk"), homeport of the Atlantic Fleet; [Naval Air Station Oceana](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Oceana "Naval Air Station Oceana"), a [master jet base](/wiki/Master_jet_base "Master jet base"); [Naval Amphibious Base Little Creek](/wiki/Naval_Amphibious_Base_Little_Creek "Naval Amphibious Base Little Creek"); and [Training Support Center Hampton Roads](/wiki/Training_Support_Center_Hampton_Roads "Training Support Center Hampton Roads") as well as a number of Navy and commercial shipyards that service navy vessels. The Aegis Training and Readiness Center is located at the [Naval Support Activity South Potomac](/wiki/Naval_Support_Activity_South_Potomac "Naval Support Activity South Potomac") in [Dahlgren, Virginia](/wiki/Dahlgren,_Virginia "Dahlgren, Virginia"). Maryland is home to [NAS Patuxent River](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Patuxent_River "Naval Air Station Patuxent River"), which houses the Navy's [Test Pilot School](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Test_Pilot_School "United States Naval Test Pilot School"). Also located in Maryland is the United States Naval Academy, situated in [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis "Annapolis"). [NS Newport](/wiki/Naval_Station_Newport "Naval Station Newport") in Newport, Rhode Island is home to many schools and tenant commands, including the [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School "Officer Candidate School"), [Naval Undersea Warfare Center](/wiki/Naval_Undersea_Warfare_Center "Naval Undersea Warfare Center"), and more, and also maintains inactive ships.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

There is also a naval base in Charleston, South Carolina. This is home to the Naval Nuclear Power Training Command, under which reside the Nuclear Field "A" Schools (for Machinist Mates (Nuclear), Electrician Mates (Nuclear), and Electronics Technicians (Nuclear)), Nuclear Power School (Officer and Enlisted); and one of two Nuclear Power Training Unit 'Prototype' schools. The state of Florida is the location of three major bases, [NS Mayport](/wiki/Naval_Station_Mayport "Naval Station Mayport"), the Navy's fourth largest, in [Jacksonville, Florida](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Florida "Jacksonville, Florida"); [NAS Jacksonville](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Jacksonville "Naval Air Station Jacksonville"), a Master Air Anti-submarine Warfare base; and [NAS Pensacola](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Pensacola "Naval Air Station Pensacola"); home of the Naval Education and Training Command, the Naval Air Technical Training Center that provides specialty training for enlisted aviation personnel and is the primary flight training base for Navy and Marine Corps [Naval Flight Officers](/wiki/Naval_Flight_Officer "Naval Flight Officer") and enlisted [Naval Aircrewmen](/wiki/Naval_aircrewman "Naval aircrewman"). There is also [NSA Panama City](/wiki/Naval_Support_Activity_Panama_City "Naval Support Activity Panama City"), Florida which is home to the Center for Explosive Ordnance Disposal and Diving (CENEODIVE) and the Navy Diving and Salvage Training Center and [NSA Orlando](/wiki/Naval_Air_Warfare_Center_Training_Systems_Division "Naval Air Warfare Center Training Systems Division"), Florida, which home to the Naval Air Warfare Center Training Systems Division (NAWCTSD).

The main U.S. Navy [submarine bases](/wiki/Submarine_base "Submarine base") on the east coast are located in [Naval Submarine Base New London](/wiki/Naval_Submarine_Base_New_London "Naval Submarine Base New London") in [Groton, Connecticut](/wiki/Groton,_Connecticut "Groton, Connecticut") and [NSB Kings Bay](/wiki/Naval_Submarine_Base_Kings_Bay "Naval Submarine Base Kings Bay") in [Kings Bay, Georgia](/wiki/Kings_Bay,_Georgia "Kings Bay, Georgia"). The [Portsmouth Naval Shipyard](/wiki/Portsmouth_Naval_Shipyard "Portsmouth Naval Shipyard") near [Portsmouth, New Hampshire](/wiki/Portsmouth,_New_Hampshire "Portsmouth, New Hampshire"),[[86]](#cite_note-87) which repairs naval submarines.[[4]](#cite_note-status-4) [NS Great Lakes](/wiki/Naval_Station_Great_Lakes "Naval Station Great Lakes"), north of Chicago, Illinois is the home of the Navy's [boot camp](/wiki/Recruit_Training_Command,_Great_Lakes,_Illinois "Recruit Training Command, Great Lakes, Illinois") for enlisted sailors.

The [Washington Navy Yard](/wiki/Washington_Navy_Yard "Washington Navy Yard") in Washington, D.C., is the Navy's oldest shore establishment and serves as a ceremonial and administrative center for the U.S. Navy, home to the chief of naval operations and numerous commands.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

### Western United States and Hawaii

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Western United States and Hawaii")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/US_Navy_SEALs_SEAL_jumps_over_side_boat.jpg/250px-US_Navy_SEALs_SEAL_jumps_over_side_boat.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_SEALs_SEAL_jumps_over_side_boat.jpg)

[Underwater Demolition Team](/wiki/Underwater_Demolition_Team "Underwater Demolition Team") members using the casting technique from a speeding boat

The U.S. Navy's largest complex is [Naval Air Weapons Station China Lake](/wiki/Naval_Air_Weapons_Station_China_Lake "Naval Air Weapons Station China Lake"), California, which covers 1.1 million acres (4,500 km2) of land, or approximately one-third of the U.S. Navy's total land holdings.[[4]](#cite_note-status-4)

[Naval Base San Diego](/wiki/Naval_Base_San_Diego "Naval Base San Diego"), California is the main homeport of the Pacific Fleet, although its headquarters is located in [Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor "Pearl Harbor"), Hawaii. [NAS North Island](/wiki/NAS_North_Island "NAS North Island") is located on the north side of [Coronado, California](/wiki/Coronado,_California "Coronado, California"), and is home to Headquarters for Naval Air Forces and Naval Air Force Pacific, the bulk of the Pacific Fleet's helicopter squadrons, and part of the West Coast [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") fleet. [NAB Coronado](/wiki/Naval_Amphibious_Base_Coronado "Naval Amphibious Base Coronado") is located on the southern end of the Coronado Island and is home to the navy's west coast SEAL teams and special boat units. NAB Coronado is also home to the [Naval Special Warfare Center](/wiki/Naval_Special_Warfare_Center "Naval Special Warfare Center"), the primary training center for SEALs.

The other major collection of naval bases on the west coast is in [Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"). Among them, [NS Everett](/wiki/Naval_Station_Everett "Naval Station Everett") is one of the newer bases and the navy states that it is its most modern facility.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

[NAS Fallon](/wiki/NAS_Fallon "NAS Fallon"), Nevada serves as the primary training ground for navy strike aircrews and is home to the [Naval Strike Air Warfare Center](/wiki/Naval_Strike_Air_Warfare_Center "Naval Strike Air Warfare Center"). Master Jet Bases are also located at [NAS Lemoore](/wiki/NAS_Lemoore "NAS Lemoore"), California, and [NAS Whidbey Island](/wiki/NAS_Whidbey_Island "NAS Whidbey Island"), Washington, while the carrier-based airborne early warning aircraft community and major air test activities are located at [NAS Point Mugu](/wiki/NAS_Point_Mugu "NAS Point Mugu"), California. The naval presence in Hawaii is centered on [NS Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Naval_Station_Pearl_Harbor "Naval Station Pearl Harbor"), which hosts the headquarters of the Pacific Fleet and many of its subordinate commands.

### United States territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: United States territories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/US_Navy_030527-N-0000X-001_The_aircraft_carrier_USS_Carl_Vinson_%28CVN_70%29_pier_side_in_Apra_Harbor%2C_Guam.jpg/250px-US_Navy_030527-N-0000X-001_The_aircraft_carrier_USS_Carl_Vinson_%28CVN_70%29_pier_side_in_Apra_Harbor%2C_Guam.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_030527-N-0000X-001_The_aircraft_carrier_USS_Carl_Vinson_(CVN_70)_pier_side_in_Apra_Harbor,_Guam.jpg)

[USS *Carl Vinson*](/wiki/USS_Carl_Vinson "USS Carl Vinson") pier side in [Apra Harbor](/wiki/Apra_Harbor "Apra Harbor"), Guam

Guam, an island strategically located in the Western Pacific Ocean, maintains a sizable U.S. Navy presence, including [NB Guam](/wiki/Naval_Base_Guam "Naval Base Guam"). The westernmost U.S. territory, it contains a natural Deepwater harbor capable of harboring aircraft carriers in emergencies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Its naval air station was deactivated[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] in 1995 and its flight activities transferred to nearby [Andersen Air Force Base](/wiki/Andersen_Air_Force_Base "Andersen Air Force Base").

[Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") in the Caribbean formerly housed [NS Roosevelt Roads](/wiki/Roosevelt_Roads_Naval_Station "Roosevelt Roads Naval Station"), which was shut down in 2004 shortly after the controversial closure of the live ordnance training area on nearby [Vieques Island](/wiki/Vieques,_Puerto_Rico "Vieques, Puerto Rico").[[4]](#cite_note-status-4)

### Foreign countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Foreign countries")]

The largest overseas base is the [United States Fleet Activities Yokosuka](/wiki/United_States_Fleet_Activities_Yokosuka "United States Fleet Activities Yokosuka"), Japan, which serves as the home port for the navy's largest forward-deployed fleet and is a significant base of operations in the Western Pacific.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

European operations revolve around facilities in Italy ([NAS Sigonella](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Sigonella "Naval Air Station Sigonella") and [Naval Computer and Telecommunications Station Naples](/wiki/Naval_Computer_and_Telecommunications_Station_Naples,_Italy "Naval Computer and Telecommunications Station Naples, Italy")) with [NSA Naples](/wiki/Naval_Support_Activity_Naples "Naval Support Activity Naples") as the homeport for the Sixth Fleet and Command Naval Region Europe, Africa, Southwest Asia (CNREURAFSWA), and additional facilities in nearby [Gaeta](/wiki/Gaeta "Gaeta"). There is also [NS Rota](/wiki/Naval_Station_Rota,_Spain "Naval Station Rota, Spain") in Spain and [NSA Souda Bay](/wiki/Crete_Naval_Base "Crete Naval Base") in Greece.

In the Middle East, naval facilities are located almost exclusively in countries bordering the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), with [NSA Bahrain](/wiki/Naval_Support_Activity_Bahrain "Naval Support Activity Bahrain") serving as the headquarters of [U.S. Naval Forces Central Command](/wiki/U.S._Naval_Forces_Central_Command "U.S. Naval Forces Central Command") and [U.S. Fifth Fleet](/wiki/U.S._Fifth_Fleet "U.S. Fifth Fleet").

[NS Guantanamo Bay](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_Naval_Base "Guantanamo Bay Naval Base") in Cuba is the oldest overseas facility and has become known in recent years as the location of a [detention camp](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_detention_camp "Guantanamo Bay detention camp") for suspected [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") operatives.[[89]](#cite_note-ForeignFacilities-90)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Main articles: [List of equipment of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of equipment of the United States Navy") and [Navy](/wiki/Navy "Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Aerial_bow_view_of_George_Washington_Carrier_Strike_Group_in_formation_for_photo_exercise_060429-N-9621S-014.jpg/250px-Aerial_bow_view_of_George_Washington_Carrier_Strike_Group_in_formation_for_photo_exercise_060429-N-9621S-014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_bow_view_of_George_Washington_Carrier_Strike_Group_in_formation_for_photo_exercise_060429-N-9621S-014.jpg)

USS *George Washington* [carrier strike group](/wiki/Carrier_strike_group "Carrier strike group") sails in formation, 29 April 2006.

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit), the navy operates over 460 ships (including vessels operated by the [Military Sealift Command](/wiki/Military_Sealift_Command "Military Sealift Command")), 3,650+ aircraft, 50,000 non-combat vehicles and owns 75,200 buildings on 3,300,000 acres (13,000 km2).

### Ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Ships")]

Main articles: [United States Navy ships](/wiki/United_States_Navy_ships "United States Navy ships") and [Future of the United States Navy](/wiki/Future_of_the_United_States_Navy "Future of the United States Navy")

See also: [List of equipment of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of equipment of the United States Navy"), [List of current ships of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_current_ships_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of current ships of the United States Navy"), [List of currently active United States military watercraft](/wiki/List_of_currently_active_United_States_military_watercraft "List of currently active United States military watercraft"), and [United States ship naming conventions](/wiki/United_States_ship_naming_conventions "United States ship naming conventions")

The names of commissioned ships of the U.S. Navy are prefixed with the letters "USS", designating "United States Ship".[[90]](#cite_note-91) Non-commissioned, civilian-manned vessels of the navy have names that begin with "USNS", standing for "United States Naval Ship". The names of ships are officially selected by the secretary of the navy, often to honor important people or places.[[91]](#cite_note-92) Additionally, each ship is given a letter-based [hull classification symbol](/wiki/Hull_classification_symbol "Hull classification symbol") (for example, CVN or DDG) to indicate the vessel's type and number. All ships in the navy inventory are placed in the [Naval Vessel Register](/wiki/Naval_Vessel_Register "Naval Vessel Register"), which is part of "the Navy List" (required by article 29 of the [United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea](/wiki/United_Nations_Convention_on_the_Law_of_the_Sea "United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea")).[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:United_States_Navy#Dubious "Talk:United States Navy")*] The register tracks data such as the current status of a ship, the date of its commissioning, and the date of its decommissioning. Vessels that are removed from the register prior to disposal are said to be *stricken* from the register. The navy also maintains a [reserve fleet](/wiki/United_States_Navy_reserve_fleets "United States Navy reserve fleets") of inactive vessels that are maintained for reactivation in times of need.

The U.S. Navy was one of the first to install [nuclear reactors aboard naval vessels](/wiki/Nuclear_marine_propulsion "Nuclear marine propulsion").[[92]](#cite_note-93) Today, [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") powers all active U.S. aircraft carriers and [submarines](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine").

In early 2010, the U.S. Navy had identified a need for 313 combat ships but could only afford 232 to 243 ships.[[93]](#cite_note-94) In March 2014, the Navy started counting self-deployable support ships such as minesweepers, surveillance craft, and tugs in the "battle fleet" to reach a count of 272 as of October 2016,[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) and it includes ships that have been put in "shrink wrap".[[96]](#cite_note-97) The number of ships generally ranged between 270 and 300 throughout the late 2010s.[[97]](#cite_note-CSR1-98) As of February 2022, the Navy has 296 battle force ships, however analyses state the Navy needs a fleet of more than 500 to meet its commitments.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100)

#### Aircraft carriers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Aircraft carriers")]

Main article: [List of aircraft carriers of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_aircraft_carriers_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of aircraft carriers of the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/USS_Gerald_R._Ford_%28CVN-78%29_and_USS_Harry_S._Truman_%28CVN-75%29_underway_in_the_Atlantic_Ocean_on_4_June_2020_%28200604-N-BD352-0199%29.JPG/250px-USS_Gerald_R._Ford_%28CVN-78%29_and_USS_Harry_S._Truman_%28CVN-75%29_underway_in_the_Atlantic_Ocean_on_4_June_2020_%28200604-N-BD352-0199%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:USS_Gerald_R._Ford_(CVN-78)_and_USS_Harry_S._Truman_(CVN-75)_underway_in_the_Atlantic_Ocean_on_4_June_2020_(200604-N-BD352-0199).JPG)

Aerial view of [USS *Gerald R. Ford*](/wiki/USS_Gerald_R._Ford "USS Gerald R. Ford") (bottom), a ship of the new [*Gerald R. Ford* class](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford-class_aircraft_carrier "Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier"), alongside [USS *Harry S. Truman*](/wiki/USS_Harry_S._Truman "USS Harry S. Truman") (top), a ship of the previous [*Nimitz* class](/wiki/Nimitz-class_aircraft_carrier "Nimitz-class aircraft carrier")

[Aircraft carriers](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") act as [airbases](/wiki/Military_airbase "Military airbase") for [carrier-based aircraft](/wiki/Carrier-based_aircraft "Carrier-based aircraft"). They are the largest vessels in the Navy fleet and all are nuclear-powered.[[97]](#cite_note-CSR1-98) An aircraft carrier is typically deployed along with a host of additional vessels, forming a [carrier strike group](/wiki/Carrier_strike_group "Carrier strike group"). The supporting ships, which usually include three or four [Aegis](/wiki/Aegis_combat_system "Aegis combat system")-equipped cruisers and destroyers, a frigate, and two attack submarines, are tasked with protecting the carrier from air, missile, sea, and undersea threats as well as providing additional strike capabilities themselves. Ready logistics support for the group is provided by a combined ammunition, oiler, and supply ship. Modern carriers are [named](/wiki/United_States_ship_naming_conventions "United States ship naming conventions") after American admirals and politicians, usually presidents.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101)

The Navy has a statutory requirement for a minimum of 11 aircraft carriers.[[101]](#cite_note-102) All 11 carriers are currently active, ten [*Nimitz*-class](/wiki/Nimitz-class_aircraft_carrier "Nimitz-class aircraft carrier") and one [*Gerald R. Ford*-class](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford-class_aircraft_carrier "Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier").

#### Amphibious warfare ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Amphibious warfare ships")]

Main article: [List of United States Navy amphibious warfare ships](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_amphibious_warfare_ships "List of United States Navy amphibious warfare ships")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/USS_Bataan_%28LHD-5%29%3B10080504.jpg/250px-USS_Bataan_%28LHD-5%29%3B10080504.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Bataan_(LHD-5);10080504.jpg)

[USS *Bataan*](/wiki/USS_Bataan_(LHD-5) "USS Bataan (LHD-5)"), a [*Wasp*-class](/wiki/Wasp-class_amphibious_assault_ship "Wasp-class amphibious assault ship") [amphibious assault ship](/wiki/Amphibious_assault_ship "Amphibious assault ship")

[Amphibious assault ships](/wiki/Amphibious_assault_ship "Amphibious assault ship") are the centerpieces of US amphibious warfare and fulfill the same power projection role as aircraft carriers except that their striking force centers on land forces instead of aircraft. They deliver, command, coordinate, and fully support all elements of a 2,200-strong [Marine Expeditionary Unit](/wiki/Marine_Expeditionary_Unit "Marine Expeditionary Unit") in an amphibious assault using both air and amphibious vehicles. Resembling small aircraft carriers, amphibious assault ships are capable of [V/STOL](/wiki/V/STOL "V/STOL"), [STOVL](/wiki/STOVL "STOVL"), [VTOL](/wiki/VTOL "VTOL"), tiltrotor, and rotary wing aircraft operations. They also contain a [well deck](/wiki/Well_deck "Well deck") to support the use of [Landing Craft Air Cushion](/wiki/Landing_Craft_Air_Cushion "Landing Craft Air Cushion") (LCAC) and other amphibious assault watercraft. Recently, amphibious assault ships have begun to be deployed as the core of an [expeditionary strike group](/wiki/Expeditionary_strike_group "Expeditionary strike group"), which usually consists of an additional [amphibious transport dock](/wiki/Amphibious_transport_dock "Amphibious transport dock") and [dock landing ship](/wiki/Dock_landing_ship "Dock landing ship") for amphibious warfare and an Aegis-equipped cruiser and destroyer, frigate, and attack submarine for group defense. Amphibious assault ships are typically named after World War II aircraft carriers.

[Amphibious transport docks](/wiki/Amphibious_transport_docks "Amphibious transport docks") are warships that embark, transport, and land Marines, supplies, and equipment in a supporting role during amphibious warfare missions. With a landing platform, amphibious transport docks also have the capability to serve as secondary aviation support for an expeditionary group. All amphibious transport docks can operate helicopters, LCACs, and other conventional amphibious vehicles while the newer *San Antonio* class of ships has been explicitly designed to operate all three elements of the Marines' "mobility triad": [Expeditionary Fighting Vehicles](/wiki/Expeditionary_Fighting_Vehicle "Expeditionary Fighting Vehicle") (EFVs), the [V-22 Osprey](/wiki/V-22_Osprey "V-22 Osprey") tiltrotor aircraft, and LCACs. Amphibious transport docks are typically named after U.S. cities.

The dock landing ship is a medium amphibious transport that is designed specifically to support and operate LCACs, though it is able to operate other amphibious assault vehicles in the United States inventory as well. Dock landing ships are normally deployed as a component of an expeditionary strike group's amphibious assault contingent, operating as a secondary launch platform for LCACs. All dock landing ships are named after cities or important places in U.S. and U.S. Naval history.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101)

The Navy operates 32 amphibious warfare ships, eight [*Wasp* class](/wiki/Wasp-class_amphibious_assault_ship "Wasp-class amphibious assault ship") and two [*America* class](/wiki/America-class_amphibious_assault_ship "America-class amphibious assault ship") amphibious assault ships, four [*Harpers Ferry* class](/wiki/Harpers_Ferry-class_dock_landing_ship "Harpers Ferry-class dock landing ship") and six [*Whidbey Island* class](/wiki/Whidbey_Island-class_dock_landing_ship "Whidbey Island-class dock landing ship") dock landing ships, and 12 [*San Antonio* class](/wiki/San_Antonio-class_amphibious_transport_dock "San Antonio-class amphibious transport dock") amphibious transport dock ships.

#### Cruisers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cruisers")]

Main article: [List of cruisers of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_cruisers_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of cruisers of the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/US_Navy_030903-N-5024R-003_USS_Port_Royal_%28DDG_73%29_departed_on_deployment.jpg/250px-US_Navy_030903-N-5024R-003_USS_Port_Royal_%28DDG_73%29_departed_on_deployment.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_030903-N-5024R-003_USS_Port_Royal_(DDG_73)_departed_on_deployment.jpg)

[USS *Port Royal*](/wiki/USS_Port_Royal_(CG-73) "USS Port Royal (CG-73)"), a [*Ticonderoga*-class](/wiki/Ticonderoga-class_cruiser "Ticonderoga-class cruiser") [cruiser](/wiki/Cruiser "Cruiser")

[Cruisers](/wiki/Cruiser "Cruiser") are large surface combat vessels that conduct anti-air/anti-missile warfare, surface warfare, anti-submarine warfare, and strike operations independently or as members of a larger task force. Modern guided missile cruisers were developed out of a need to counter the [anti-ship missile](/wiki/Anti-ship_missile "Anti-ship missile") threat facing the United States Navy. This led to the development of the [AN/SPY-1](/wiki/AN/SPY-1 "AN/SPY-1") phased array radar and the [RIM-67 Standard](/wiki/RIM-67_Standard "RIM-67 Standard") missile with the Aegis combat system coordinating the two. [*Ticonderoga*-class](/wiki/Ticonderoga-class_cruiser "Ticonderoga-class cruiser") cruisers were the first to be equipped with Aegis and were put to use primarily as anti-air and anti-missile defense in a battle force protection role. Later developments of [vertical launch systems](/wiki/Vertical_launch_system "Vertical launch system") and the [Tomahawk missile](/wiki/BGM-109_Tomahawk "BGM-109 Tomahawk") gave cruisers additional long-range land and sea strike capability, making them capable of both offensive and defensive battle operations. The *Ticonderoga* class is the only active class of cruiser. All cruisers in this class are named after battles.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101)

#### Destroyers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Destroyers")]

Main article: [List of destroyers of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_destroyers_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of destroyers of the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/DDG-125_acceptance_trials.jpg/250px-DDG-125_acceptance_trials.jpg)](/wiki/File:DDG-125_acceptance_trials.jpg)

[USS *Jack H. Lucas*](/wiki/USS_Jack_H._Lucas "USS Jack H. Lucas"), a [*Arleigh Burke*-class destroyer](/wiki/Arleigh_Burke-class_destroyer "Arleigh Burke-class destroyer")

[Destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") are multi-mission medium surface ships capable of sustained performance in anti-air, anti-submarine, anti-ship, and offensive strike operations. Like cruisers, guided missile destroyers are primarily focused on surface strikes using Tomahawk missiles and fleet defense through Aegis and the Standard missile. Destroyers additionally specialize in anti-submarine warfare and are equipped with [VLA rockets](/wiki/ASROC "ASROC") and [LAMPS Mk III Sea Hawk](/wiki/Sikorsky_SH-60_Seahawk "Sikorsky SH-60 Seahawk") helicopters to deal with underwater threats. When deployed with a carrier strike group or expeditionary strike group, destroyers and their fellow Aegis-equipped cruisers are primarily tasked with defending the fleet while providing secondary strike capabilities. With very few exceptions, destroyers are named after U.S. Navy, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard heroes.[[100]](#cite_note-:1-101)

The U.S. Navy currently has 75 destroyers, 73 [*Arleigh Burke*-class](/wiki/Arleigh_Burke-class_destroyer "Arleigh Burke-class destroyer") destroyers and two [*Zumwalt*-class](/wiki/Zumwalt-class_destroyer "Zumwalt-class destroyer") stealth destroyers, with a third (the [USS *Lyndon B. Johnson*](/wiki/USS_Lyndon_B._Johnson "USS Lyndon B. Johnson")) expected to enter service sometime in 2024.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

#### Frigates and Littoral combat ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Frigates and Littoral combat ships")]

Main articles: [List of frigates of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_frigates_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of frigates of the United States Navy") and [Littoral combat ship](/wiki/Littoral_combat_ship "Littoral combat ship")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/USS_Independence_%28LCS-2%29_at_Naval_Air_Station_Key_West_on_29_March_2010_%28100329-N-1481K-298%29.jpg/250px-USS_Independence_%28LCS-2%29_at_Naval_Air_Station_Key_West_on_29_March_2010_%28100329-N-1481K-298%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Independence_(LCS-2)_at_Naval_Air_Station_Key_West_on_29_March_2010_(100329-N-1481K-298).jpg)

[USS *Independence*](/wiki/USS_Independence_(LCS-2) "USS Independence (LCS-2)"), a [littoral combat ship](/wiki/Littoral_combat_ship "Littoral combat ship")

Modern U.S. [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") mainly perform anti-submarine warfare for carrier and expeditionary strike groups and provide armed escort for supply convoys and merchant shipping. They are designed to protect friendly ships against hostile submarines in low to medium threat environments, using torpedoes and LAMPS helicopters. Independently, frigates are able to conduct counterdrug missions and other maritime interception operations. As in the case of destroyers, frigates are named after U.S. Navy, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard heroes.

In late 2015, the U.S. Navy retired its most recent class of traditional frigates in favor of the littoral combat ship (LCS), relatively small vessels designed for near-shore operations that was expected to assume many of the duties the frigate had with the fleet. The LCS was "envisioned to be a networked, agile, stealthy surface combatant capable of defeating [anti-access](/wiki/Anti-access/area_denial "Anti-access/area denial") and [asymmetric threats](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare") in the [littorals](/wiki/Littoral_zone "Littoral zone")",[[103]](#cite_note-104) although their ability to perform these missions in practice has been called into question.[[104]](#cite_note-105) The Navy has announced it plans to reduce procurement of the LCS and retire early examples of the type.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/USS_Constitution_fires_a_17-gun_salute.jpg/250px-USS_Constitution_fires_a_17-gun_salute.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Constitution_fires_a_17-gun_salute.jpg)

[USS *Constitution*](/wiki/USS_Constitution "USS Constitution"), firing a 17-gun salute in 2014

The Navy planned to purchase up to 20 of the [*Constellation*-class frigate](/wiki/Constellation-class_frigate "Constellation-class frigate") (18 cancelled 2 under construction to be completed).[[105]](#cite_note-USNI_Constell_cancel-106) *Constellation* is based on the [FREMM multipurpose frigate](/wiki/FREMM_multipurpose_frigate "FREMM multipurpose frigate"),[[105]](#cite_note-USNI_Constell_cancel-106) already in service with European navies.

The U.S. Navy has 23 littoral combat ships, eight [*Freedom*-class](/wiki/Freedom-class_littoral_combat_ship "Freedom-class littoral combat ship") and 15 [*Independence*-class](/wiki/Independence-class_littoral_combat_ship "Independence-class littoral combat ship") ships as of 2025.

#### Mine countermeasures ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Mine countermeasures ships")]

Main article: [List of mine warfare vessels of the United States Navy](/wiki/List_of_mine_warfare_vessels_of_the_United_States_Navy "List of mine warfare vessels of the United States Navy")

[Mine countermeasures vessels](/wiki/Mine_countermeasures_vessel "Mine countermeasures vessel") are a combination of [minehunters](/wiki/Minehunter "Minehunter"), a naval vessel that actively detects and destroys individual [naval mines](/wiki/Naval_mine "Naval mine"), and [minesweepers](/wiki/Minesweeper "Minesweeper"), which clear mined areas as a whole, without prior detection of the mines. MCM vessels have mostly legacy names of previous US Navy ships, especially World War II-era minesweepers.

The Navy operates eight [*Avenger*-class mine countermeasures ships](/wiki/Avenger-class_mine_countermeasures_ship "Avenger-class mine countermeasures ship"), with four expected to be retired in 2024.

#### Submarines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Submarines")]

Main article: [Submarines in the United States Navy](/wiki/Submarines_in_the_United_States_Navy "Submarines in the United States Navy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/USS_Kentucky_%28SSBN-737%29.jpg/250px-USS_Kentucky_%28SSBN-737%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Kentucky_(SSBN-737).jpg)

[USS *Kentucky*](/wiki/USS_Kentucky_(SSBN-737) "USS Kentucky (SSBN-737)"), an [*Ohio*-class](/wiki/Ohio-class_submarine "Ohio-class submarine") [ballistic missile submarine](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine")

The U.S. Navy operates three types of submarines: [attack submarines](/wiki/Attack_submarine "Attack submarine"), [ballistic missile submarines](/wiki/Ballistic_missile_submarine "Ballistic missile submarine") and [guided missile submarines](/wiki/Guided_missile_submarine "Guided missile submarine"). All current and planned U.S. Navy submarines are nuclear-powered, as nuclear propulsion allows for a combination of stealth and long-duration, high-speed, sustained underwater movement.

Attack submarines typically operate as part of a [carrier battle group](/wiki/Carrier_battle_group "Carrier battle group"), while [guided missile submarines](/wiki/Guided_missile_submarine "Guided missile submarine") generally operate independently and carry larger quantities of cruise missiles. Both types have several tactical missions, including sinking ships and other subs, launching [cruise missiles](/wiki/Cruise_missile "Cruise missile"), gathering intelligence, and assisting in special operations. Ballistic missile submarines operate independently with only one mission: to carry and, if called upon, to launch the [Trident nuclear missile](/wiki/Trident_nuclear_missile "Trident nuclear missile").

The Navy operates 69 submarines, 29 [*Los Angeles* class](/wiki/Los_Angeles-class_submarine "Los Angeles-class submarine") attack submarines (with two more in reserve), 18 [*Ohio* class](/wiki/Ohio-class_submarine "Ohio-class submarine") submarines with 14 configured as ballistic missile submarines and four configured as guided missile submarines, three [*Seawolf* class](/wiki/Seawolf-class_submarine "Seawolf-class submarine") attack submarines, and 19 [*Virginia* class](/wiki/Virginia-class_submarine "Virginia-class submarine") attack submarines.

#### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Other")]

A special case is the [USS *Constitution*](/wiki/USS_Constitution "USS Constitution"), commissioned in 1797 as one of the original six frigates of the United States Navy and which remains in commission at the Charlestown Navy Yard in Boston. She occasionally sails for commemorative events such as [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)").

The Navy operates a class of small tugboats, called [barrier boats](/wiki/Barrier_Boat "Barrier Boat").

### Aircraft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Aircraft")]

Main article: [List of active United States naval aircraft](/wiki/List_of_active_United_States_naval_aircraft "List of active United States naval aircraft")

See also: [List of United States Navy aircraft squadrons](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_aircraft_squadrons "List of United States Navy aircraft squadrons") and [List of United States Navy aircraft designations (pre-1962)](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_aircraft_designations_(pre-1962) "List of United States Navy aircraft designations (pre-1962)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Four_Super_Hornets.jpg/250px-Four_Super_Hornets.jpg)](/wiki/File:Four_Super_Hornets.jpg)

Four Navy [F/A-18E/F Super Hornets](/wiki/F/A-18E/F_Super_Hornet "F/A-18E/F Super Hornet")

Carrier-based aircraft are able to strike air, sea, and land targets far from a carrier strike group while protecting friendly forces from enemy aircraft, ships, and submarines. In peacetime, aircraft's ability to project the threat of sustained attack from a mobile platform on the seas gives United States leaders significant diplomatic and crisis-management options. Aircraft additionally provide logistics support to maintain the navy's readiness and, through helicopters, supply platforms with which to conduct [search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue"), [special operations](/wiki/Special_operations "Special operations"), anti-submarine warfare (ASW), and anti-surface warfare, including the U.S. Navy's premier Maritime Strike and only organic ASW aircraft, the venerable Sikorsky MH-60R operated by [Helicopter Maritime Strike Wing](/wiki/Helicopter_Maritime_Strike_Wing "Helicopter Maritime Strike Wing").

The U.S. Navy began to research the use of aircraft at sea in the 1910s, with Lieutenant Theodore G. "Spuds" Ellyson becoming the first naval aviator on 28 January 1911, and commissioned its first aircraft carrier, [USS *Langley* (CV-1)](/wiki/USS_Langley_(CV-1) "USS Langley (CV-1)"), in 1922.[[106]](#cite_note-107) United States naval aviation fully came of age in World War II, when it became clear following the attack on Pearl Harbor, the Battle of the Coral Sea, and the Battle of Midway that aircraft carriers and the planes that they carried had replaced the battleship as the greatest weapon on the seas. Leading navy aircraft in World War II included the [Grumman F4F Wildcat](/wiki/Grumman_F4F_Wildcat "Grumman F4F Wildcat"), the [Grumman F6F Hellcat](/wiki/Grumman_F6F_Hellcat "Grumman F6F Hellcat"), the Chance [Vought F4U Corsair](/wiki/Vought_F4U_Corsair "Vought F4U Corsair"), the [Douglas SBD Dauntless](/wiki/Douglas_SBD_Dauntless "Douglas SBD Dauntless"), and the [Grumman TBF Avenger](/wiki/Grumman_TBF_Avenger "Grumman TBF Avenger"). Navy aircraft also played a significant role in conflicts during the following Cold War years, with the [F-4 Phantom II](/wiki/F-4_Phantom_II "F-4 Phantom II") and the [F-14 Tomcat](/wiki/F-14_Tomcat "F-14 Tomcat") becoming military icons of the era. The navy's current primary fighter-attack airplane is the multi-mission [F/A-18E/F Super Hornet](/wiki/F/A-18E/F_Super_Hornet "F/A-18E/F Super Hornet"). The [F-35C](/wiki/F-35_Lightning_II "F-35 Lightning II") entered service in 2019.[[107]](#cite_note-108) The Navy is also looking to eventually replace its F/A-18E/F Super Hornets with the [F/A-XX](/wiki/F/A-XX "F/A-XX") program.

The Aircraft Investment Plan sees naval aviation growing from 30 percent of current aviation forces to half of all procurement funding over the next three decades.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

### Weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Weapons")]

Main article: [List of United States Navy weapons](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_weapons "List of United States Navy weapons")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/US_Navy_050112-N-5345W-074_Aviation_Ordnancemen_prepare_to_load_500-pound_laser_guided_bombs_%28GBU-12%29_onto_weapon_pylons_under_an_F-14B_Tomcat.jpg/250px-US_Navy_050112-N-5345W-074_Aviation_Ordnancemen_prepare_to_load_500-pound_laser_guided_bombs_%28GBU-12%29_onto_weapon_pylons_under_an_F-14B_Tomcat.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_050112-N-5345W-074_Aviation_Ordnancemen_prepare_to_load_500-pound_laser_guided_bombs_(GBU-12)_onto_weapon_pylons_under_an_F-14B_Tomcat.jpg)

Aviation Ordnancemen loading [GBU-12](/wiki/GBU-12_Paveway_II "GBU-12 Paveway II") bombs in 2005

Current U.S. Navy shipboard weapons systems are almost entirely focused on missiles, both as a weapon and as a threat. In an offensive role, missiles are intended to strike targets at long distances with accuracy and precision. Because they are unmanned weapons, missiles allow for attacks on heavily defended targets without risk to human pilots. Land strikes are the domain of the BGM-109 Tomahawk, which was first deployed in the 1980s and is continually being updated to increase its capabilities. For anti-ship strikes, the navy's dedicated missile is the [Harpoon Missile](/wiki/Harpoon_Missile "Harpoon Missile"). To defend against enemy missile attack, the navy operates a number of systems that are all coordinated by the Aegis combat system. Medium-long range defense is provided by the Standard Missile 2, which has been deployed since the 1980s. The Standard missile doubles as the primary shipboard anti-aircraft weapon and is undergoing development for use in theater ballistic missile defense. Short range defense against missiles is provided by the [Phalanx CIWS](/wiki/Phalanx_CIWS "Phalanx CIWS") and the more recently developed [RIM-162 Evolved Sea Sparrow Missile](/wiki/ESSM "ESSM"). In addition to missiles, the navy employs [Mark 46](/wiki/Mark_46_torpedo "Mark 46 torpedo"), [Mark 48](/wiki/Mark_48_torpedo "Mark 48 torpedo"), and [Mark 50](/wiki/Mark_50_torpedo "Mark 50 torpedo") [torpedoes](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo") and various types of naval mines.

Naval fixed-wing aircraft employ much of the same weapons as the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") for both air-to-air and air-to-surface combat. Air engagements are handled by the heat-seeking [Sidewinder](/wiki/AIM-9_Sidewinder "AIM-9 Sidewinder") and the radar guided [AMRAAM](/wiki/AIM-120_AMRAAM "AIM-120 AMRAAM") missiles along with the [M61 Vulcan](/wiki/M61_Vulcan "M61 Vulcan") cannon for close range dogfighting. For surface strikes, navy aircraft use a combination of missiles, smart bombs, and dumb bombs. On the list of available missiles are the [Maverick](/wiki/AGM-65_Maverick "AGM-65 Maverick"), [SLAM-ER](/wiki/AGM-84H/K_SLAM-ER "AGM-84H/K SLAM-ER") and [JSOW](/wiki/AGM-154_Joint_Standoff_Weapon "AGM-154 Joint Standoff Weapon"). Smart bombs include the GPS-guided [JDAM](/wiki/JDAM "JDAM") and the laser-guided [Paveway](/wiki/Paveway "Paveway") series. Unguided munitions such as dumb bombs and [cluster bombs](/wiki/Cluster_bomb "Cluster bomb") make up the rest of the weapons deployed by fixed-wing aircraft.

Rotary aircraft weapons are focused on anti-submarine warfare (ASW) and light to medium surface engagements. To combat submarines, helicopters use Mark 46 and Mark 50 torpedoes. Against small watercraft, they use [Hellfire](/wiki/AGM-114_Hellfire "AGM-114 Hellfire") and [Penguin](/wiki/Penguin_missile "Penguin missile") air to surface missiles. Helicopters also employ various types of mounted anti-personnel machine guns, including the [M60](/wiki/M60_machine_gun "M60 machine gun"), [M240](/wiki/M240_machine_gun "M240 machine gun"), [GAU-16](/wiki/GAU-16 "GAU-16")/A, and [GAU-17/A](/wiki/Minigun "Minigun").

Nuclear weapons in the U.S. Navy arsenal are deployed through ballistic missile submarines and aircraft. The *Ohio*-class submarine carries the latest iteration of the [Trident missile](/wiki/Trident_missile "Trident missile"), a three-stage, [submarine-launched ballistic missile](/wiki/Submarine-launched_ballistic_missile "Submarine-launched ballistic missile") (SLBM) with [MIRV](/wiki/MIRV "MIRV") capability; the current Trident II (D5) version is expected to be in service past 2020.[[109]](#cite_note-110) The navy's other nuclear weapon is the air-deployed [B61 nuclear bomb](/wiki/B61_nuclear_bomb "B61 nuclear bomb"). The B61 is a thermonuclear device that can be dropped by strike aircraft such as the F/A-18 Hornet and Super Hornet at high speed from a large range of altitudes. It can be released through free-fall or parachute and can be set to detonate in the air or on the ground.

## Naval jack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Naval jack")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Jack_of_the_United_States.svg/250px-Jack_of_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jack_of_the_United_States.svg)

U.S. naval jack

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Naval_jack_of_the_United_States_%282002%E2%80%932019%29.svg/250px-Naval_jack_of_the_United_States_%282002%E2%80%932019%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Naval_jack_of_the_United_States_(2002%E2%80%932019).svg)

First navy jack

The current [naval jack](/wiki/Jack_(flag) "Jack (flag)") of the United States is the [Union Jack](/wiki/Jack_of_the_United_States "Jack of the United States"), a small blue flag emblazoned with the stars of the 50 states. The Union Jack was not flown for the duration of the war on terror, during which Secretary of the Navy [Gordon R. England](/wiki/Gordon_R._England "Gordon R. England") directed all U.S. naval ships to fly the [First Navy Jack](/wiki/First_Navy_Jack "First Navy Jack"). While Secretary England directed the change on 31 May 2002, many ships chose to shift colors later that year in remembrance of the first anniversary of the [September 11, 2001 attacks](/wiki/September_11,_2001_attacks "September 11, 2001 attacks"). The Union Jack, however, remained in use with vessels of the U.S. Coast Guard and [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration"). A jack of similar design to the Union Jack was used in 1794, with 13 stars arranged in a 3–2–3–2–3 pattern. When a ship is moored or anchored, the jack is flown from the [bow](/wiki/Bow_(watercraft) "Bow (watercraft)") of the ship while the ensign is flown from the [stern](/wiki/Stern "Stern"). When underway, the ensign is raised on the mainmast. Before the decision for all ships to fly the First Navy Jack, it was flown only on the oldest ship in the active American fleet, which is currently [USS *Blue Ridge*](/wiki/USS_Blue_Ridge_(LCC-19) "USS Blue Ridge (LCC-19)"). U.S. Navy ships and craft returned to flying the Union Jack effective 4 June 2019. The date for reintroduction of the jack commemorates the Battle of Midway, which began on 4 June 1942.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

## Naval post offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Navy&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Naval post offices")]

During World War I the first U.S. government post offices were established aboard Navy ships, managed by a Navy postal clerk. Prior to this, mail from crew members was collected and at the first opportunity was dropped off at a port of call where it was processed at a US Post Office. Before the arrival of email and the internet, hand stamped mail was the only way Navy crew members at sea could communicate with their family, friends and others. Mail was considered almost as valuable to crew members as food and ammunition.[[116]](#cite_note-117) Sometimes mail from various crew members (referred to by historians and collectors as [postal history](/wiki/Postal_history "Postal history")), is directly associated with naval history.[[117]](#cite_note-118) Letters and other correspondence sent by commanders, officers and crew members can include names, ranks, signatures, addresses, and ship's postmarks which can often confirm dates and locations of naval ships and crew members during various battles or other naval operations. As such, naval mail can serve as a source of information to naval historians and biographers. Among the more notable examples of Naval postal history include letters sent from the [USS *Arizona*](/wiki/USS_Arizona "USS Arizona"), before and on 7 December 1941.[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120)[[120]](#cite_note-121)
